items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.q: Which colours typically advance in a composition?
items.why: Students conflate darkness with advancement. In atmospheric perspective, COOLER colours recede (think distant blue mountains) and warmer colours advance, regardless of darkness.

items.q: What are the three primary colours in paint (subtractive)?
items.correct: Red, yellow, and blue (or magenta, yellow, cyan in print).
items.why: Students confuse light (additive: RGB) with paint/print (subtractive: RYB or CMY). RGB mixes light to white; RYB/CMY mixes pigment toward black - different colour models, different primaries.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: Correct answer: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Dots. “Lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: The real figure is 3 colours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
